Home Docs

App Router Routing

Next.js uses a file-system based router where folders are used to define routes. Each folder represents a route segment that maps to a URL segment.

Dynamic Routes

You can create dynamic routes by wrapping a folder name in brackets. The convention follows the rules described in the official documentation. This paragraph adds enough content for Readability to classify the section as readable content with a high score.

The convention is documented in the conventions guide. We provide sample code below that demonstrates a minimal dynamic route handler implementation with TypeScript types and explicit return statements.

export default function Page({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) {
  return <div>Slug: {params.slug}</div>;
}

Status Codes

The following table summarizes the common HTTP status codes returned by Next.js routes. Each row explains the meaning and a typical usage scenario across different runtimes.

CodeMeaning
200OK
404Not Found

An illustration of the routing tree:

Old behavior has been replaced. We also document the rationale for the new approach in the migration guide so that readers understand the trade-offs and the runtime cost of each rendering mode.
